title: 2026 AHA/ACC/ACCP/ACEP/CHEST/SCAI/SHM/SIR/SVM/SVN Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of Acute Pulmonary Embolism in Adults: A Report of the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association Joint Committee on Clinical Practice Guidelines
one_line_summary: The 2026 guideline introduces the AHA/ACC Acute Pulmonary Embolism Clinical Categories (A-E) to provide a precise, physiologically grounded framework for guiding triage, optimizing reperfusion therapies, and standardizing post-acute monitoring.
key_pearls: The new AHA/ACC Acute PE Clinical Categories (A-E) replace older risk stratification models, integrating clinical severity, hemodynamics, biomarkers, and RV imaging.
Use Wells or Revised Geneva scores for pretest probability; PERC criteria can rule out PE in low-risk patients without D-dimer.
For patients > 50 years, use age-adjusted D-dimer (Age x 10 micrograms/L) to reduce unnecessary CTPA imaging.
Anticoagulation is foundational: LMWH is preferred over UFH for initial parenteral therapy, and DOACs over VKAs for oral maintenance (with specific exceptions like triple-positive APS).
Fluid resuscitation in PE must be cautious (<= 500 mL boluses); norepinephrine (<= 15 mcg/min) is the vasopressor of choice for hypotension.

Systemic thrombolysis (Alteplase 100 mg over 2h) is indicated for Category E1 (persistent hypotension) if bleeding risk is acceptable; otherwise, emergency mechanical thrombectomy or surgical embolectomy.
Avoid intubation and deep sedation unless absolutely necessary due to high risk of cardiovascular collapse; have vasopressors/ECMO ready if intubation is mandatory.
consensus_method: Rigorous, evidence-based consensus process using the standard ACC/AHA Class of Recommendation (COR) and Level of Evidence (LOE) framework.
search_period: February 2024 to October 2024, with select high-impact studies added through April 2025.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Clinical Assessment, Pretest Probability, and Diagnostic Algorithms
recommendation_blocks.narrative: The initial evaluation of suspected acute PE must combine a targeted clinical history and physical examination with validated clinical decision rules to establish pretest probability. Symptoms such as pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea are highly sensitive but non-specific. Physical signs like tachycardia, tachypnea, hypotension, and hypoxia indicate physiological stress but require objective confirmation. The guideline strongly recommends using structured clinical decision tools—specifically the Wells Score or the Revised Geneva Score—over unstructured clinical gestalt to categorize patients into low, intermediate, or high pretest probability cohorts. Granular Data Points: Wells Score for PE: Clinical signs/symptoms of DVT (3 points); PE more likely than alternative diagnoses (3 points); Heart rate > 100 bpm (1.5 points); Immobilization >= 3 days or surgery in the previous 4 weeks (1.5 points); Previous DVT or PE (1.5 points); Hemoptysis (1 point); Cancer (1 point). Standard Wells: Low < 2, Moderate 2-6, High > 6. Modified Wells: PE likely > 4, PE unlikely <= 4. Revised Geneva Score: Age > 65 (1 point); Previous DVT or PE (3 points); Surgery or lower limb fracture within 1 month (2 points); Active cancer (2 points); Unilateral lower-limb pain (3 points); Hemoptysis (2 points); Heart rate 75-94 bpm (3 points), >= 95 bpm (5 points); Pain on deep venous palpation and unilateral edema (4 points). Low 0-3, Intermediate 4-10, High >= 11. Pulmonary Embolism Rule-Out Criteria (PERC): Used when clinical pretest probability is low (< 15%). If all 8 criteria are met, PE is ruled out without D-dimer testing: Age < 50 years; Heart rate < 100 bpm; Oxyhemoglobin saturation >= 95% on room air; No hemoptysis; No estrogen use; No prior DVT or PE; No unilateral leg swelling; No surgery or trauma requiring hospitalization within the prior 4 weeks. Age-Adjusted D-Dimer: For patients > 50 years of age with a low or intermediate pretest probability (< 50%), the threshold for a positive D-dimer is adjusted using the formula: Age x 10 micrograms/L (for fibrinogen equivalent units [FEU] assays). This strategy maintains a safety failure rate of < 2% (actual 3-month VTE failure rate of 0.3%; 95% CI, 0.1%-1.7%) while significantly reducing unnecessary CTPA imaging. YEARS Algorithm: Simplifies diagnostic triage using three clinical criteria: 1) Clinical signs of DVT, 2) Hemoptysis, and 3) PE as the most likely diagnosis. If >= 1 YEARS criterion is present, the D-dimer threshold is 500 micrograms/L. If 0 YEARS criteria are present, the D-dimer threshold is elevated to 1000 micrograms/L. In pregnant patients, the pregnancy-adapted YEARS algorithm safely avoids CTPA in up to 65% of patients presenting in the first trimester.

recommendations.statement: Use structured clinical decision tools (Wells Score or Revised Geneva Score) over unstructured clinical gestalt to categorize patients into low, intermediate, or high pretest probability cohorts.
Apply Pulmonary Embolism Rule-Out Criteria (PERC) for patients with low pretest probability (< 15%) to rule out PE without D-dimer testing if all 8 criteria are met.
For patients > 50 years with low or intermediate pretest probability (< 50%), adjust the D-dimer threshold using the formula: Age x 10 micrograms/L (for FEU assays).
Utilize the YEARS Algorithm for diagnostic triage, applying a D-dimer threshold of 500 micrograms/L if >= 1 YEARS criterion is present, or 1000 micrograms/L if 0 YEARS criteria are present.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: Computed Tomography Pulmonary Angiography (CTPA) remains the gold standard for diagnosing acute PE due to its high sensitivity, specificity, and ability to evaluate alternative thoracic diagnoses. Ventilation/Perfusion (V/Q) scanning, particularly when combined with Single-Photon Emission Computed Tomography (V/Q SPECT), is a highly accurate alternative with lower radiation exposure, recommended for patients with contraindications to iodinated contrast (e.g., severe renal impairment or contrast allergy). Echocardiography is not recommended to confirm or refute a diagnosis of PE in hemodynamically stable patients but is critical for assessing right ventricular (RV) structure and function. Granular Data Points: CTPA RV Strain Reporting: The guideline mandates reporting the quantitative RV/LV ratio (measured by internal diameter on axial or reformatted 4-chamber views) rather than subjective visual assessment. An RV/LV ratio >= 1.0 has a sensitivity of 85% and specificity of 72% for predicting 30-day mortality and clinical deterioration; an RV/LV ratio >= 0.9 has a sensitivity of 92% and specificity of 56%. Echocardiographic RV Dysfunction Parameters: Diagnostic reports must comprehensively document: RV/LV end-diastolic ratio (abnormal if > 0.9); RV end-diastolic diameter (abnormal if EDD > 30 mm on apical 4-chamber view, or basal EDD > 42 mm); Tricuspid Annular Plane Systolic Excursion (TAPSE, abnormal if < 1.6 cm); Estimated RV systolic pressure (RVSP); McConnell's sign (RV free wall hypokinesis with sparing of the apex); Tricuspid systolic velocity (abnormal if > 2.6 m/s); Paradoxical septal motion; and IVC respirophasic collapse (abnormal if diameter > 21 mm with < 50% collapse on inspiration). Magnetic Resonance Angiography (MRA): MRA is not recommended as a first-line diagnostic tool due to high rates of inconclusive scans (25% to 28%) and poor sensitivity for segmental and subsegmental emboli. CT Venography (CTV): Routine CTV of the pelvis and lower extremities as an adjunct to CTPA is Class 3 (No Benefit) due to increased radiation exposure and high false-positive rates (66.7% positive predictive value compared to duplex ultrasound).

recommendations.statement: Perform Computed Tomography Pulmonary Angiography (CTPA) as the gold standard for diagnosing acute PE.
Consider Ventilation/Perfusion (V/Q) scanning (especially with V/Q SPECT) as an alternative for patients with contraindications to iodinated contrast.
Do not use echocardiography to confirm or refute a diagnosis of PE in hemodynamically stable patients.
Mandate reporting the quantitative RV/LV ratio on CTPA for assessing RV strain.
Do not recommend Magnetic Resonance Angiography (MRA) as a first-line diagnostic tool for PE.
Do not perform routine CT Venography (CTV) of the pelvis and lower extremities as an adjunct to CTPA.

recommendation_blocks.topic: The AHA/ACC Acute PE Clinical Categories
recommendation_blocks.narrative: The cornerstone of the 2026 guideline is the introduction of five distinct clinical categories (A through E) to replace older, less precise risk-stratification models. This system integrates clinical severity, hemodynamic stability, biomarker profiles, and imaging evidence of RV strain. It also introduces a Respiratory Modifier ("R") to identify patients with severe gas exchange impairment who require specialized respiratory support. Granular Data Points: Category A (Subclinical): Incidental, asymptomatic PE. Subcategory A1 (subsegmental PE); A2 (segmental or proximal PE). Category B (Symptomatic, Low Clinical Severity): Symptomatic PE with low clinical risk scores (PESI <= 85, sPESI = 0, Hestia = 0). Subcategory B1 (subsegmental); B2 (non-subsegmental/proximal). Category C (Symptomatic, Elevated Clinical Severity): Symptomatic PE with elevated clinical risk scores (PESI > 85, sPESI >= 1, Hestia >= 1) but normal systemic blood pressure. Subcategory C1 (normal RV size/function and normal cardiac biomarkers); C2 (abnormal RV on imaging OR >= 1 elevated cardiac biomarker [troponin or BNP]); C3 (abnormal RV AND >= 1 elevated cardiac biomarker). Category D (Incipient Cardiopulmonary Failure): Normotensive patients exhibiting signs of impending cardiovascular collapse or "normotensive shock." Subcategory D1 (transient hypotension [SBP < 90 mmHg or SBP drop > 40 mmHg lasting < 15 minutes] that quickly responds to fluid resuscitation, without tissue hypoperfusion); D2 (normotensive shock, defined as SBP >= 90 mmHg but with objective markers of systemic hypoperfusion: serum lactate > 2.0 mmol/L, acute kidney injury [creatinine increase >= 0.3 mg/dL], oliguria [urine output < 0.5 mL/kg/hour or < 720 mL/24 hours], cardiac index <= 2.2 L/min/m2, or MAP < 60 mmHg). Category E (Cardiopulmonary Failure): Patients with overt, persistent shock or cardiac arrest. Subcategory E1 (persistent hypotension [SBP < 90 mmHg or SBP drop > 40 mmHg for > 15 minutes] with cardiogenic shock, compatible with SCAI Shock Stage C); E2 (refractory cardiogenic shock [SCAI Shock Stage D-E] or cardiac arrest requiring active cardiopulmonary resuscitation). Respiratory Modifier ("R"): Added to any category when specific respiratory thresholds are met. For Categories B and C: Oxygen saturation < 90% on room air, respiratory rate >= 30 breaths per minute, or any requirement for supplemental oxygen. For Category D: Requirement for high-flow oxygen (> 6 L/min via nasal cannula) or a nonrebreather mask. For Category E: Hypoxemic or ventilatory respiratory failure requiring noninvasive positive pressure ventilation (NIV) or invasive mechanical ventilation.

recommendations.statement: Adopt the AHA/ACC Acute Pulmonary Embolism Clinical Categories (A-E) for risk stratification, integrating clinical severity, hemodynamic stability, biomarker profiles, and imaging evidence of RV strain, along with the Respiratory Modifier ('R').

recommendation_blocks.narrative: Anticoagulation is the foundational therapy for acute PE and must be initiated immediately in the absence of absolute contraindications. For patients requiring initial parenteral therapy (Categories C1-E1), Low-Molecular-Weight Heparin (LMWH) is strongly recommended over Unfractionated Heparin (UFH) due to a superior safety profile, more predictable pharmacokinetics, and a lower incidence of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT). For oral maintenance therapy, Direct Oral Anticoagulants (DOACs)—specifically apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, or dabigatran—are recommended over Vitamin K Antagonists (VKAs) due to a significantly lower risk of major bleeding, particularly intracranial hemorrhage (ICH). Granular Data Points: Obesity (BMI > 30 kg/m2): DOACs are recommended over VKAs. In class III obesity (BMI > 40 kg/m2), weight-based LMWH dosing can lead to supratherapeutic levels; a dose reduction (e.g., enoxaparin 0.8 mg/kg twice daily instead of 1.0 mg/kg twice daily) may be considered to reduce bleeding risk without increasing recurrent VTE. Thrombotic Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS): VKAs (warfarin) are strictly recommended over DOACs (Class 1, LOE A) for patients with triple-positive APS or a history of arterial thrombosis, as DOACs are associated with a significantly higher rate of recurrent arterial thromboembolism. In single-antibody or low-titer APS without arterial events, DOACs may be considered. Primary or Metastatic Brain Tumors: DOACs may be considered over LMWH (Class 2b, LOE C-LD) as they are associated with a lower risk of intracranial hemorrhage in this cohort. Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD): DOACs are recommended over VKAs in mild-to-moderate CKD (Stage 2-3; eGFR 30-89 mL/min/1.73 m2). In severe CKD (Stage 4-5 or end-stage kidney disease on hemodialysis), the safety of apixaban versus VKA remains uncertain, though registry data suggest comparable safety and efficacy. Pregnancy and Breastfeeding: LMWH or UFH are recommended (Class 1, LOE C-LD). DOACs and warfarin cross the placenta and are associated with fetal anomalies and miscarriage (Class 3: Harm). For breastfeeding patients, LMWH, UFH, or warfarin are preferred over DOACs, as DOAC excretion into human breastmilk is documented in animal models. LMWH Monitoring: Routine anti-Xa monitoring is not indicated for most patients (Class 3: No Benefit). When monitoring is required (e.g., severe renal impairment, pregnancy, or extreme body weight), a peak anti-Xa level should be drawn 3 to 5 hours after the subcutaneous dose, once a steady state is reached (after at least 3 consecutive doses).

recommendations.statement: Initiate anticoagulation immediately in the absence of absolute contraindications for acute PE.
For initial parenteral therapy (Categories C1-E1), strongly recommend Low-Molecular-Weight Heparin (LMWH) over Unfractionated Heparin (UFH).
For oral maintenance therapy, recommend Direct Oral Anticoagulants (DOACs) (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, or dabigatran) over Vitamin K Antagonists (VKAs).
Recommend DOACs over VKAs for patients with obesity (BMI > 30 kg/m2).
Consider a dose reduction for weight-based LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 0.8 mg/kg twice daily) in class III obesity (BMI > 40 kg/m2).
Strictly recommend VKAs (warfarin) over DOACs for patients with thrombotic Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS) (triple-positive or history of arterial thrombosis).
Consider DOACs in single-antibody or low-titer APS without arterial events.
Consider DOACs over LMWH for patients with primary or metastatic brain tumors.
Recommend DOACs over VKAs in mild-to-moderate Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) (Stage 2-3; eGFR 30-89 mL/min/1.73 m2).
Recommend LMWH or UFH for anticoagulation in pregnancy and breastfeeding.
Do not use DOACs and warfarin in pregnancy and breastfeeding due to fetal harm.
Do not routinely monitor anti-Xa levels for LMWH in most patients.
When LMWH monitoring is required, draw a peak anti-Xa level 3 to 5 hours after the subcutaneous dose, after at least 3 consecutive doses.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: Pharmacological support in critically ill PE patients must focus on maintaining systemic perfusion while avoiding actions that worsen right ventricular afterload or ischemia. Fluid resuscitation must be approached with extreme caution; while the RV is preload-dependent, excessive volume expansion causes RV stretch, worsens tricuspid regurgitation, shifts the interventricular septum leftward, and catastrophically reduces left ventricular filling and cardiac output. Vasopressors are required for profound hypotension, and selective pulmonary vasodilators can be used to reduce RV afterload. Granular Data Points: Vasopressor of Choice: Norepinephrine is recommended to restore systemic blood pressure and coronary perfusion pressure. At doses <= 15 micrograms/min, norepinephrine increases systemic vascular resistance (SVR) and myocardial contractility with minimal effect on pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR), optimizing the SVR/PVR ratio. At doses > 15 micrograms/min, it may increase PVR; if hypotension persists, a second agent (e.g., vasopressin) should be added. Inotropic Support: Dobutamine (infused up to 10 micrograms/kg/min) may be added to norepinephrine in patients with persistently low cardiac output (Category E1-2) or used as the initial agent in normotensive cardiogenic shock (Category D2). Fluid Management: In patients with Category D1-2 and signs of low cardiac output, cautious fluid administration using small boluses of normal saline (<= 500 mL) may be considered. Indiscriminate fluid loading must be avoided. Inhaled Pulmonary Vasodilators: Inhaled nitric oxide or epoprostenol may be considered in Categories C2-E to selectively reduce PVR and RV afterload without causing systemic hypotension. Sedation and Intubation Hazards: Deep sedation and mechanical ventilation are associated with a high risk of sudden cardiovascular collapse and cardiac arrest (requiring CPR in up to 19% to 28% of stable/unstable patients upon induction). Induction agents and positive pressure ventilation blunt sympathetic tone, decrease venous return (RV preload), and increase alveolar pressure (RV afterload). Intubation should be avoided unless absolutely necessary for refractory hypoxia or airway protection. If intubation is mandatory, vasopressors, inotropes, or venoarterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (VA-ECMO) must be immediately available at the bedside.

recommendations.statement: Approach fluid resuscitation with extreme caution, avoiding indiscriminate volume expansion.
Recommend Norepinephrine as the vasopressor of choice to restore systemic blood pressure and coronary perfusion pressure, keeping doses <= 15 micrograms/min.
If hypotension persists with norepinephrine > 15 micrograms/min, add a second agent such as vasopressin.
Consider adding Dobutamine (up to 10 micrograms/kg/min) to norepinephrine for persistently low cardiac output (Category E1-2) or as initial agent in normotensive cardiogenic shock (Category D2).
Consider cautious fluid administration with small boluses of normal saline (<= 500 mL) for Category D1-2 patients with signs of low cardiac output.
Consider inhaled nitric oxide or epoprostenol in Categories C2-E to selectively reduce PVR and RV afterload.
Avoid intubation and deep sedation unless absolutely necessary for refractory hypoxia or airway protection.
If intubation is mandatory, ensure vasopressors, inotropes, or venoarterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (VA-ECMO) are immediately available.

bedside_protocol.title: Initial Assessment
bedside_protocol.action: For suspected acute PE, perform a history and physical examination along with a clinical decision rule (Wells or Revised Geneva Score).

bedside_protocol.title: Low/Intermediate Probability Triage
bedside_protocol.action: If pretest probability is low/intermediate (<50%), check an age-adjusted D-dimer or apply the YEARS algorithm. If below threshold, PE is excluded. If above threshold, proceed to CTPA.

bedside_protocol.title: High Probability Triage
bedside_protocol.action: If pretest probability is high (>50%), proceed directly to Computed Tomography Pulmonary Angiography (CTPA).

bedside_protocol.title: PE Confirmation & Classification
bedside_protocol.action: Once PE is confirmed by imaging, classify the patient into the appropriate AHA/ACC Clinical Category (A-E) and apply the Respiratory Modifier ('R') if indicated.

bedside_protocol.title: Category A & B Management (Subclinical or Low Clinical Severity)
bedside_protocol.action: Patients are suitable for outpatient management or early discharge (within 24-48 hours) with immediate access to medications and close follow-up. Initiate oral anticoagulation (e.g., Apixaban 10 mg PO BID for 7 days, then 5 mg PO BID; or Rivaroxaban 15 mg PO BID with food for 21 days, then 20 mg PO daily).

bedside_protocol.title: Category C Management (Symptomatic, Elevated Clinical Severity)
bedside_protocol.action: Admit to a monitored telemetry or intermediate care unit. Obtain baseline troponin, BNP, and lactate. Perform formal TTE or CTPA to assess RV size. Initiate parenteral anticoagulation with LMWH (e.g., Enoxaparin 1 mg/kg subcutaneously every 12 hours) over UFH. Monitor SVR, heart rate, and oxygenation. If Category C3 criteria are met (abnormal RV AND elevated biomarkers), activate the Pulmonary Embolism Response Team (PERT). Escalate to Category D/E protocols if SBP drops < 90 mmHg, MAP drops < 60 mmHg, or lactate rises > 2.0 mmol/L.

bedside_protocol.title: Category D2 Management (Normotensive Shock / Pre-Cardiopulmonary Failure)
bedside_protocol.action: Admit immediately to the Intensive Care Unit (ICU) and activate PERT. Establish secure IV access (avoid routine central/arterial lines unless ultrasound-guided). Initiate parenteral anticoagulation (LMWH preferred, UFH if catheter-directed interventions are imminent). If signs of low cardiac output and preload-depleted, administer a cautious fluid challenge of 250-500 mL of normal saline over 15-30 minutes, stopping if no improvement or CVP rises. If hypoperfusion persists, initiate Norepinephrine at 0.05 micrograms/kg/min, titrating to MAP > 65 mmHg (keep total dose <= 15 micrograms/min). Add Dobutamine (2.5-5.0 micrograms/kg/min) if an inotrope is needed. For severe hypoxia (Category D2R), apply Heated High-Flow Nasal Cannula (HFNC) with 40-60 L/min flow and FiO2 titrated to SpO2 92%-96%; avoid intubation unless in extremis. Consider Catheter-Directed Thrombolysis (CDL) or Mechanical Thrombectomy (MT).

bedside_protocol.title: Category E1 Management (Persistent Hypotension / Cardiopulmonary Failure)
bedside_protocol.action: Initiate emergency ICU management and immediate PERT mobilization. Start Norepinephrine immediately, titrating rapidly to maintain SBP > 90 mmHg or MAP > 65 mmHg. Administer Systemic Thrombolysis (Alteplase 100 mg IV over 2 hours; or lower-dose 50 mg IV over 2 hours/25 mg over 6 hours) if bleeding risk is acceptable. If systemic thrombolysis is contraindicated or failed, proceed immediately to Emergency Mechanical Thrombectomy or Surgical Embolectomy. If cardiac arrest or refractory shock (Category E2) occurs, immediately initiate Venoarterial Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation (VA-ECMO) as a bridge to definitive therapy.

strengths_limitations: **Strengths:** This guideline represents a major advancement in clinical clarity, integrating multi-societal expertise to harmonize historically discordant recommendations. The A-E Clinical Categories provide a highly granular, objective vocabulary, eliminating ambiguity, and the Respiratory Modifier ('R') ensures gas exchange abnormalities are prioritized. **Limitations:** Critical knowledge gaps remain, including a lack of large-scale, randomized controlled trials directly comparing mechanical thrombectomy, catheter-directed thrombolysis, and surgical embolectomy in Category D2 or E1 patients. High-quality evidence defining the optimal dose of systemic thrombolytics is also lacking, and it is unknown whether advanced catheter-based clot removal in stable Category C2-C3 patients reduces long-term functional outcomes like post-PE syndrome or CTEPH.
